summary: Gruber recounts a frustrating experience using his power company PECO's terrible mobile website during a 26-hour outage, only to discover during a subsequent outage that their native iOS app was surprisingly good. He uses this contrast as evidence that the mobile web is languishing relative to native apps, arguing that the gap between them is widening rather than narrowing. He notes the irony that a utility company — the last place you'd expect to need a native app — delivers a dramatically better experience through its app than its website. He concludes that while the web overall may not be dying, the mobile web specifically appears to be in decline.
key_points: PECO's mobile website was painfully slow, buggy, confusing to navigate, and aggressively signed users out, while their native iOS app was fast, well-organized, and stayed logged in
The expectation that utility companies should prioritize web over native apps is outdated — the native app experience has surpassed mobile web even for services where apps seem unnecessary
The claim that mobile web apps are closing the gap with native apps is contradicted by real-world experience; the gulf is widening
The trend of calling websites 'desktop apps' signals both the decline of native Mac apps and the decline of building good mobile web experiences
Even cross-platform app frameworks deliver a meaningfully better experience than mobile web, though iOS still outperforms Android in this case
money_quotes: Basically, PECO's mobile website feels like it was developed using, and exported from, Microsoft Excel. You might say, 'Well that makes no sense, because you've never been able to build or export websites using Excel.' To which I'd respond, 'Yes, exactly.'
And the notion that mobile web apps are closing the gap with native apps is laughable. The gulf between them is widening, not narrowing.

I feel like a chump for having suffered through the previous 26-hour outage obsessively checking their terrible, slow-loading (I just can't emphasize how fucking slow it is), broken website when this app was available.

If one of the two should suck, it should be the app that sucks and the website that works well. You shouldn't be expected to carry around a bundle of software from your utility company in your pocket.
I won't hold up this one experience as a sign that the web is dying, but it sure seems to be languishing, especially for mobile devices.

I suspect that my instinctive belief that a service company or utility should focus its customer service efforts on the web first, and native apps second, is every bit as outdated as my stubborn belief that invite ought not be used as a noun.
I'd have given PECO some credit for honesty if they'd simply replaced the estimated time for power restoration with the shrug emoji.
key_insight: The widening gap between native mobile apps and mobile web experiences — visible even in mundane utility company software — suggests the mobile web is languishing while native apps continue to improve.
